content.full_text:
CITY OF OAKLAND

Sep 14 9 59 JUL'92

CITY HALL • ONE CITY HALL PLAZA'92 OAKLAND, CALIFORNIA 94612
(415) 273-3601
TTY 839-6451

Office of the City Attorney
Jayne W. Williams
City Attorney

September 14, 1992


Mr. Scott Hallabrin
Counsel, Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J. Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, Ca. 95804

Re:  Request for Advice, Government Code section 83114(b)
     Councilmember Frank Ogawa

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

I. INTRODUCTION

     I am requesting written advice on behalf of Oakland City
Councilmember Frank Ogawa.  Councilmember Ogawa has advised the
Oakland City Attorney's Office that the residence which he owns is
not located within 300 feet of the boundaries of the proposed boundaries of the proposed Dunsmuir Heights project but
that it is located within a radius 2,500 feet of the boundaries or
the proposed boundaries of the proposed Dunsmuir Heights Project.

     Councilmember Ogawa has provided us with a letter dated
July 10, 1992, from Max Morris of Adams, Adams & Morris, a copy of
which is attached, advising him that in his opinion as a real
estate broker that the building of the Dunsmuir Heights Project
will not directly or indirectly influence the value or
marketability of his residence.  Mr. Morris indicates that he is an
experienced real estate broker including having the experience of
marketing a 20 acre development site in the immediate area of the
proposed Dunsmuir Heights Project.  Mr. Morris opines that "...the
building of the Dunsmuir Heights Project will not directly or
indirectly influence the value or marketability of Councilman
Ogawa's residence.  Due to the distance, location and topography of
the proposed project it will not benefit him in the form of
improved storm drains, utility services or any other secondary
benefits that [he] could foresee."

     In a letter dated August 10, 1992 from Max A. Morris to
Councilmember Ogawa, a copy of which is attached, Mr. Morris
further states that based upon his experience as a real estate
broker for several years in the East Bay area and from his


Mr. Scott Hallabrin
September 14, 1992
Page 2

experience of marketing a 20 acre development site in the immediate
area of the proposed development known as the Dunsmuir Heights
Project that, "[t]here is no reasonably foreseeable financial
benefit or potential loss in value that would affect the home owned
by Councilman Ogawa as a result of building the Dunsmuir Heights
Project".

II. ISSUE PRESENTED

     If Max Morris, a real estate broker opines that there is
no reasonably foreseeable financial benefit or potential loss in
value to Councilmember Ogawa's residence as a result of the
proposed Dunsmuir Heights project, would Councilmember Ogawa who
owns a residence which is not located within a radius of 300 feet
of boundaries or the proposed boundaries of the proposed Dunsmuir
Heights project but that is located within a radius of 2,500 feet
of the boundaries or the proposed boundaries of the proposed
Dunsmuir Heights Project have a conflict of interest if he makes,
influences or participates in decisions regarding the Dunsmuir
Heights project?

III. BACKGROUND

     Hayward Exchange, Inc. applied for a preliminary Planned
Unit Development (PUD) Permit from the City of Oakland to develop
the 132-acre Dunsmuir Heights property. Initially, the applicant
submitted a preliminary PUD Plan that showed 507 dwelling units and
related public and private infrastructure improvements. Also, the
applicant applied for a general plan amendment to delete a required
through street connection and a development agreement to allow
project build out without being affected by local land use changes
that occur subsequent to project approval.

     The general plan amendment, which was a prerequisite to
the other approvals, was granted by the City Council, as
recommended by the City Planning Commission, earlier this year.
Councilmember Ogawa did not participate in that action, because of
the location of his home in proximity to the through road that was
the subject of the general plan amendment and the potential that he
had a conflict of interest.

     The PUD and Development Agreement applications are still
pending before the Planning Commission. The PUD application has
been revised to show 341 residential units with related
infrastructure. The Commission is scheduled to take a final vote
of approval on the PUD on September 16, 1992. It will vote on the
Development agreement on September 30, 1992. Under the City's
zoning regulations, any interested party can appeal the Planning

Mr. Scott Hallabrin
September 14, 1992
Page 3


Commission's decision to the City Council, if such appeal is made
within 10 days of the Commission's action.  The Commission's
actions on the Development Agreement are advisory to the City
Council, since a legislative act (approval of the agreement) is
involved. As such, the commission's decision automatically goes to
the City Council.  The City Council's actions on the PUD and
Development Agreement are final. If the project is approved, there
is a related environmental impact report for the project that will
have to be certified by the Planning Commission and City Council.

     The proposed project will be located in the Oakland hills
northeast of Interstate Route 580 between Sheffield Village, the
Dunsmuir House and Gardens and the Oakland Municipal Golf Course.
The project will be composed of single family detached and duplex
market rate units.

     Finally, it is anticipated that the project, if approved,
will involve subsequent applications for tree removal permits,
subdivisions, encroachment permits and design review. The City
Council, under local regulations, could be involved in the granting
of all of these permits. The project could also involve use of the
City's eminent domain powers, as certain lands needed for street
improvements are not currently owned by the applicant.

IV.  LEGAL ANALYSIS

     Under  the  Fair  Political  Practices  Commission's
materiality regulation regarding real property in which an official
has a direct ownership interest, a decision would be material to
the official's real property, if the official's interest is located
outside a radius of 300 feet and any part of the real property is
located within a radius of 2,500 feet of the boundaries or the
proposed boundaries of the property which is the subject of the
decision and the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable
effect of: 1.) $10,000 or more on the fair market value of the real
property in which the official has an interest; or 2.) will affect
the rental value of the property by $1000 or more per 12 month
period. (Cal. Code of Reg., tit. 2, § 18702.3 (a) (3).)

     Max Morris, a real estate broker in the firm of Adams,
Adams & Morris of Castro Valley, California, has opined that there
is no reasonably foreseeable financial benefit or potential loss in
value to Councilmember Ogawa's home as a result of building the
Dunsmuir Heights Project.  We, therefore, conclude that although
Councilmember Ogawa owns property which is located outside a radius
of 300 feet but within a radius of 2,500 feet of the boundaries or
proposed boundaries of the Dunsmuir Heights Project, his interest
in this real property is not material and disqualifying because Max
Morris has concluded in his professional opinion that the Dunsmuir

Mr. Scott Hallabrin
September 14, 1992
Page 4


Heights Project will have no reasonably foreseeable financial
benefit or loss to Councilmember Ogawa's property.

V.     CONCLUSION

     Please provide us with written advice at your earliest
possible convenience so that we can advise Councilmember Ogawa of
whether he may influence, make or participate in the decision
making process regarding the Dunsmuir Heights Project.

     If we can provide you with further information, please do
not hesitate to contact us.


                                        Very truly yours,

                                        JAYNE W. WILLIAMS
                                        City Attorney

                                        [signature]
                                        By: Joyce M. Hicks
                                            Assistant City Attorney

Enclosures

H:\JWH\CONFLICT\OGAFPPC.L1

ADAMS • ADAMS
& MORRIS INC.

3541 Jamison Way, Suite 130, Castro Valley, CA 94546
(510) 537-4314

July 10, 1992


Mr. Frank Ogawa
Oakland City Council
11041 Ettrick Street
Oakland, CA 94605

RE: Disputing The Possibility Of A Conflict Of Interest
When Voting As A City Council Member On A Potential
Development Site Near Councilman's Place Of Residence

Dear Councilman Ogawa:

Based upon my experience as a real estate broker for
several years and from the experience of marketing a 20
acre development site in the immediate area of the
proposed development known as the Dunsmuir Heights Project
I submit the following opinions:

(1) It is my opinion that the building of the
Dunsmuir Heights Project will not directly
or indirectly influence the value or
marketability of Councilman Ogawa's residence.
Due to distance, location and topography of
the proposed project it will not benefit
Councilman Ogawa in the form of improved
roads, storm drains, utility services or
any other secondary benefits that I can
foresee.

(2) I would like to address the issue that Councilman
Ogawa's residence is within the 2500 foot radius
of the proposed project. I have reviewed THE
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION'S REGULATIONS
ON THIS ISSUE. The guidelines very specifically
state that the 2500 foot radius requirement is
subject to the following criteria:

The official living within the 2500 foot
radius may vote for the project if:

(a) No reasonably foreseeable financial
benefit of more than $10,000.00 to
.the official's property.

In my opinion Councilman Ogawa's
property would not benefit from
this project.

Residential • Commercial • Investment Real Estate • Property Management

Mr. Frank Ogawa
Oakland City Councilman
11041 Ettrick Street
Oakland, CA 94605

Page 2

(b) The effect of any economic benefit,
if any, would be the same for at
least 25% of all the properties in
the 2500 foot radius.

Councilman Ogawa's residence meets
with this criteria.

(c) There are a minimum of at least 10
properties under separate ownership
within the 2500 foot radius.

Councilman Ogawa's residence meets
with this criteria.

Per the attached Section 18702.3 of the regulations of THE
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION, TITLE 2, DIVISION OF
THE CALIFORNIA CODE OF REGULATIONS.

An official is not disqualified from participation
in a governmental decision if the decision affects
the official's interest in a manner which is not
distinguishable from the manner in which the decision
will affect the public generally.

After reviewing the financial issues of Councilman Ogawa's
residence and the codes provided by THE FAIR POLITICAL
PRACTICES COMMISSION, I have determined that there is no
conflict of interest or material gain for Councilman Ogawa
if this project was to be completed.

Sincerely,

Max A. Morris

Enc: California Code Regulation 18702.3

MAM;ey

(Regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission, Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations)


18702.3.  Material Financial Effect: Ownership Interest in
Real Property Indirectly Involved in the Decision

     (a)  The effect of a decision is material as to real

property in which an official has a direct, indirect or beneficial

ownership interest (not including a leasehold interest), if any of

the following applies:

     (1)  The real property in which the official has an

interest, or any part of that real property, is located within a

300 foot radius of the boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of

the property which is the subject of the decision, unless the

decision will have no financial effect upon the official's real

property interest.

     (2)  The decision involves construction of, or

improvements to, streets, water, sewer, storm drainage or similar

facilities, and the real property in which the official has an

interest will receive new or substantially improved services.

     (3)  The real property in which the official has an

interest is located outside a radius of 300 feet and any part of

the real property is located within a radius of 2,500 feet of the

boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the property which is

the subject of the decision and the decision will have a

reasonably foreseeable financial effect of:

     (A)  Ten thousand dollars ($10,000) or more on the fair

market value of the real property in which the official has an

interest; or


1                    18702.3

(B) Will affect the rental value of the property by
$1,000 or more per 12 month period.

(b) The reasonably foreseeable effect of a decision is
not considered material as to real property in which an official
has a direct, indirect or beneficial interest (not including a
leasehold interest), if the real property in which the official
has an interest is located entirely beyond a 2,500 foot radius of
the boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the property which
is the subject of the decision; unless:

(1) There are specific circumstances regarding the
decision, its effect, and the nature of the real property in which
the official has an interest, which make it reasonably foreseeable
that the fair market value or the rental value of the real
property in which the official has an interest will be affected by
the amounts set forth in subdivisions (a)(3)(A) or (a)(3)(B); and

(2) Either of the following apply:

(A) The effect will not be substantially the same as
the effect upon at least 25+percent of all the properties which
are within a 2,500 foot radius of the boundaries of the real
property in which the official has an interest; or

(B) There are not at least 10 properties under separate
ownership within a 2,500 foot radius of the property in which the
official has an interest.

(c) For decisions which may affect an interest in real
property but which do not involve a subject property from which
the distances prescribed in subdivisions (a) and (b) can be

2                                18702.3

determined, the monetary standards contained in subdivision (a)(3)

(A) and (B) shall be applied..

(d) For a decision which is covered by subdivision

(a)(3) or (b)(1) or (c), factors which shall be considered in

determining whether the decision will have the effects set forth

in subdivision (a)(3)(A) or (B) include, but are not limited to:

(1) The proximity of the property which is the subject

of the decision and the magnitude of the proposed project or

change in use in relationship to the property in which the

official has an interest;

(2) Whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the

decision will affect the development potential or income producing

potential of the property;

(3) In addition to the foregoing, in the case of

residential property, whether it is reasonably foreseeable that

the decision will result in a change to the character of the

neighborhood including, but not limited to, effects on traffic,

view, privacy, intensity of use, noise levels, air emissions, or

similar traits of the neighborhood.

(e) Redevelopment Decisions: For purposes of this

section "the boundaries (or proposed boundaries) of the property

which is the subject of the decision" are the boundaries (or

proposed boundaries) of the redevelopment project area whenever

the decision is a redevelopment decision to designate the survey

area, to make findings of blight, to select the project area, to

adopt the preliminary plan, to form a project area committee, to

certify the environmental document, to adopt the redevelopment

3                                                                          18702.3

plan, to add territory to the redevelopment area, or to rescind or
amend any of the above decisions.

Note: Authority cited: Section 83112, Government Code.
Reference: Section 87103, Government Code.

History

(1) New section filed 10-17-88; effective thirtieth day
thereafter.


4                                                    18702.3

ADAMS • ADAMS
& MORRIS INC.

3541 Jamison Way, Suite 130, Castro Valley, CA 94546
(510) 537-4314


August 10, 1992

Mr. Frank Ogawa
Oakland City Council
11041 Ettrick Street
Oakland, CA 94605

RE: Contesting The Possibility Of A Conflict Of Interest
When Voting As A City Council Member On A Potential
Development Site Near Councilman's Place Of Residence

Dear Councilman Ogawa:

Based upon my experience as a real estate broker for several
years in the East Bay area and from the experience of marketing
a 20 acre development site in the immediate area of the proposed
development known as the Dunsmuir Heights Project I submit the
following opinion:

There is no reasonably foreseeable financial benefit
or potential loss in value that would affect the home
owned by Councilman Ogawa as a result of building the
Dunsmuir Heights Project.

As I see no gain or loss from the Project to Councilman Ogawa, I
would consider him perfectly safe within the $10,000 maximum
guideline provided by the State.

Sincerely,

Max A. Morris

MAM:ey


Residential • Commercial • Investment Real Estate • Property Management

sections.conclusion:
Please provide us with written advice at your earliest possible convenience so that we can advise Councilmember Ogawa of whether he may influence, make or participate in the decision making process regarding the Dunsmuir Heights Project.

If we can provide you with further information, please do not hesitate to contact us.
